name: Launch Services
summary:
Launch Services represents the foundational infrastructure layer of the Space Economy, currently dominated by SpaceX's Falcon 9 reusable architecture which has fundamentally reset the cost-per-kilogram paradigm and captured the majority of global orbital launch market share. The narrative has shifted from a fragmented field of dozens of small-launch startups—many of which have failed, pivoted, or consolidated—to a bifurcated market: SpaceX at scale on one end, and a narrowing set of viable competitors (Rocket Lab, ULA, Blue Origin, Arianespace) competing for the remaining government, defense, and commercial demand. The sector is transitioning from a supply-constrained market to one where launch capacity is increasingly abundant, shifting value capture downstream toward satellite operators, constellation builders, and in-space services.

The critical forward-looking variable is SpaceX's Starship: if it achieves operational reliability at projected payload fractions and cadence, it compresses the competitive window for medium-lift vehicles and could make most dedicated small-launch economics structurally unviable. Concurrently, demand-side inflection is being driven by mega-constellations (Starlink, Kuiper, OneWeb), national security space expansion, and emerging civil space programs in India, China, and the Middle East. The launch services narrative is thus less about 'who can build a rocket' and more about who can achieve reusability-driven unit economics, sustained cadence, and integration with downstream space infrastructure.
key_drivers: SpaceX Falcon 9 reusability and high launch cadence resetting global price floor and capturing dominant market share
Starship development trajectory—success would restructure heavy-lift economics and compress medium-lift competitor viability
Rocket Lab's evolution from dedicated smallsat launcher to medium-lift (Neutron) as the primary credible non-SpaceX growth story
Mega-constellation deployment demand (Starlink, Project Kuiper, government constellations) as the dominant demand-side pull
Small-launch market consolidation—dozens of startups have failed or pivoted, concentrating remaining capital and contracts among survivors
National security space and government anchor contracts (NASA CLPS, DoD NSSL Phase 3) providing non-market-price revenue floors
Geopolitical fragmentation: China, India, and emerging space programs developing independent launch capacity, creating non-interoperable market segments
Shift from supply-constrained to capacity-abundant market, pushing value capture downstream toward satellite operators and in-orbit services

adoption_curve.stage: Early Majority (for reusable medium-lift); Late Innovator/Early Adopter (for heavy-lift reuse and small-launch market)
adoption_curve.growth_rate: 20-30% annually in launch attempts; revenue growth lower at 10-15% due to price compression
inflection_points: Starship achieving reliable orbital operations and high cadence—would trigger repricing of entire launch market and potential obsolescence of expendable medium-lift
Rocket Lab Neutron first commercial flights (2025-2026)—determines whether a second credible reusable medium-lift competitor emerges
Project Kuiper launch campaign commencement—creates large non-Starlink demand block requiring dozens of launches over compressed timeframe
DoD NSSL Phase 3 contract awards—locks in government revenue allocation among ULA, SpaceX, Blue Origin, and potentially Rocket Lab for the next decade
